data.title: Palm oil ends lower on weak demand
data.description:
Malaysian palm oil futures extended losses for a second straight session on Thursday as sluggish demand pressured prices.

The benchmark palm oil contract FCPO1! for March delivery on the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives Exchange slid 59 ringgit, or 1.36%, to 4,295 ringgit ($954.44) a metric ton at the close.

The contract lost 0.25% on Wednesday.

Traders are awaiting signs of market recovery after the recent rout, however, demand remains weak and continues to put pressure on prices, said Paramalingam Supramaniam, director at Selangor-based brokerage Pelindung Bestari.

"Authorities in Indonesia have also been looking into ways to curb used cooking oil exports, but the extent of the tightening is not immediately clear."

Indonesia has curbed exports of used cooking oil and palm oil residue to ensure supply to domestic cooking oil and biodiesel industries, the government said on Wednesday.

Cargo surveyors are scheduled to release Jan. 1-10 export data, and the Malaysia Palm Oil Board will release its December supply-demand data, both on Friday.

Dalian's most-active soyoil contract (DBYcv1) fell 1.16%, while its palm oil contract CPO1! lost 3.09%. Soyoil prices on the Chicago Board of Trade ZL1! were down 0.77%.

Palm oil tracks price movements of rival edible oils as it competes for a share of the global vegetable oils market.

Oil prices were little changed, with investors weighing firm winter fuel demand expectations against large builds of fuel inventories in the U.S., the world's biggest oil user, and macroeconomic concerns. O/R

Weaker crude oil futures make palm a less attractive option for biodiesel feedstock.

The ringgit USDMYR, palm's currency of trade, remained unchanged against the U.S dollar.

($1 = 4.5000 ringgit)

data.title: COMMENT-Profit taking could save South Africa's rand
data.description:
From a technical perspective the daily USD/ZAR chart is showing the potential for a treble top at 18.99-19.00 and profit taking on dollar longs ahead of the developing resistance might trigger a stronger rand recovery.

Emerging markets continue to be driven by all things U.S. and strong data, sticky inflation and doubts over the Federal Reserve's easing cycle will continue to shape market action. However, if the current bond market storm calms and dollar demand begins to moderate there could be room for emerging market currencies like the rand to recover.

USD/ZAR peaked at 19.0050 and 19.00 back in June 2024, just shy of the April 2024 high of 19.3800. Thursday's rally put in a high of 18.99 before fading. A modest pullback to 18.9100 seen so far but the market looks stretched, and a deeper drop might be on the cards if profit taking becomes heavier.

However, a climb above the 19.00 level and close above Wednesday's 18.9700 high could see the dollar push on through to the 19.38 April high before the market turns.

For more click on

data.title: UK CHANCELLOR RACHEL REEVES EXPECTED TO TAKE URGENT PARLIAMENTARY QUESTIONS ON RISING GILT YIELDS AT 10:30 GMT
Paul Krugman thinks bond yields may be rising due to an 'insanity premium'
data.description:
By William Watts

Investors debate how market is digesting Donald Trump's policy pronouncements ahead of inauguration

Investors are debating what competing, or perhaps complementary, factors are driving a rise in Treasury yields that's served to rattle the stock market.

Recently retired New York Times columnist - and Nobel-winning economist - Paul Krugman offered up via his new Substack newsletter what he sees as a possible explanation.

"[I]ncreases in long-term rates, like the 10-year Treasury rate, might reflect the horrible, creeping suspicion that Donald Trump actually believes the crazy things he says about economic policy and will act on those beliefs," he wrote in a post titled, "Is There an Insanity Premium on Interest Rates?"

Krugman, of course, is no Trump fan, nor vice versa. But questions around exactly what the Trump agenda will entail are certainly in focus among investors as his Jan. 20 inauguration approaches.

For his part, Krugman suspects the market is reacting to the president-elect's pronouncements on tariffs, while also noting his refusal to rule out economic or military coercion to gain control of Greenland and the Panama Canal, while referring to Canada as the "51st state."

See: 3 reasons Trump keeps floating U.S. control of Greenland - including one tied to tax cuts

Krugman cited a "near-unanimity" among economists that Trump's agenda of high tariffs, tax cuts and mass deportations would be highly inflationary, albeit "probably not right away."

"Still, if he were to go through with any substantial part of that agenda, the Fed would definitely have to put further interest-rate cuts on hold. In fact, it might well feel the need to raise rates again," Krugman wrote.

Minutes of the Federal Reserve's December policy meeting echoed remarks made by Chair Jerome Powell at the time. "Almost all" Fed officials judged that the upside risks to inflation had increased, the minutes said, due in part to potential changes in trade and immigration policies under consideration by the incoming Trump administration. Those concerns didn't stop the Fed from delivering a quarter-point rate cut last month.

Earlier Wednesday, economist Adam Posen, an expert on monetary policy who served on the Bank of England's rate-setting committee, said he thought the Fed would have to start hiking rates this summer in response to Trump's budget plans.

However, investors on Wednesday also heard from Fed Gov. Christopher Waller, who said he supported more interest-rate cuts this year and didn't think that proposed import tariffs from the incoming Trump administration would lead to persistent, upward pressure on inflation.

Investors aren't ready to attribute all of the yield rise to jitters around tariffs and other policies. Indeed, Krugman's post came in response to a note on Tuesday from Torsten Slok, chief economist at Apollo Global Management, who termed the jump in the 10-year Treasury yield BX:TMUBMUSD10Y from around 3.6% in September to near 4.7% highly unusual following a Fed rate cut.

"Is it fiscal worries? Is it less demand from abroad? Or maybe Fed cuts were not justified? The market is telling us something, and it is very important for investors to have a view on why long rates are going up when the Fed is cutting," Slok wrote.

Investors and analysts continue to debate the potential impact of Trump's tariff plans, whether they remain a negotiating tactic and exactly how much impact they will ultimately have on prices.

Meanwhile, whatever the reason for the rise, the jump in yields is getting the blame for sapping a post-election stock-market rally, particularly given lofty valuations for high-flying tech stocks. Equities put in a mixed performance Wednesday, but stumbled at the end of a stellar 2024. The S&P 500 SPX is down around 0.4% so far this week, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA off 0.2% and the Nasdaq Composite COMP declining 0.6%.

"I think the Greenland thing is probably more entertainment than it is fundamental," said Mark Hackett, chief of investment research at Nationwide, in a phone interview.

Tariff concerns, in contrast, are having an impact, he said, but argued that equities are a "market in a selloff-in-search-of-a-reason mode right now."

In other words, after a run that saw the S&P 500 log a second straight 20%-plus annual gain, the market is "expensive and tired," with tariff jitters a handy excuse for selling.

When it comes to the rise in yields, Krugman acknowledged that his favored explanation "may be wishful thinking." The economist predicted an imminent global recession when Trump was first elected president in November 2016, but retracted the call a few days later, warning that Trump's victory could eventually have dire effects but could serve to accelerate near-term economic growth.

Krugman in Wednesday's post said he didn't want to push his argument "too far, in part because I don't want to give in to motivated reasoning. Those of us horrified by Trump's ascent would like to see him punished by the markets, but we shouldn't expect instant gratification. It may well be years before the consequences of his economic delusions are really obvious."

-Greg Robb contributed.

-William Watts

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Market Chatter: BYD Accused of Importing Chinese Labor to Brazil on Irregular Visas
data.description:
BYD 002594 allegedly brought hundreds of workers from China on irregular visas to construct a factory in Brazil, Reuters reported, citing a labor inspector.

Authorities rescued 163 of the workers in December from "slavery-like conditions," the report said.

The rescued workers, said to be recruited by BYD contractor Jinjiang, are about to leave or have left Latin America's largest country, the report said.

BYD could be fined for every worker found in slavery-like conditions, Reuters reported, citing Liane Durao, an inspector under Brazil's labor ministry.

BYD said it has already severed ties with Jinjiang, while a source close to the carmaker said the work visas were properly issued, and the employees flew voluntarily to Brazil, the report said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Universal Robina Buys Back More Shares
data.description:
Philippine food and beverage group Universal Robina URC bought back another 185,000 shares, according to a Thursday Philippine bourse filing.

The shares were repurchased at prices ranging from 77.40 Philippines pesos to 78.70 pesos apiece.

So far, the company has spent 5.82 billion pesos of the 8 billion pesos it allocated for the buyback program.

data.title: Bond Markets Are Open. Why Long-Term Yields Are a Nightmare for Stocks. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Brian Swint

The stock market may be closed on Thursday, but all the real action this week is happening in bonds anyway.

The biggest story in finance is that longer-term bond yields are rising. The yield on the 10-year Treasury, at about 4.7%, is the highest since April, even though the Federal Reserve's overnight rate is now a percentage point lower than it was then. The 30-year Treasury yield has jumped even further, reaching the highest in more than a year.

Ultimately, the rise in longer-term interest rates will be bad for stocks. Shares are valued relative to their returns to bonds, so if bonds start returning more, stocks become relatively less attractive. It might be a moment for investors to ask if former Fed Chair Alan Greenspan's decades-old warning of "irrational exuberance" should apply now, too.

The stock market is doing fine for now. The Dow Jones Industrial Average and S&P 500 rose on Wednesday, and the Nasdaq fell slightly. All three are still up slightly in the few trading days of 2025 so far. The stock exchange is closed to commemorate President Jimmy Carter on Thursday. The bond market will close early at 2 p.m. Eastern time.

The 10-year Treasury yield stood at 4.681% early Thursday. The two-year yield was at 4.291%, both down slightly from a day ago. Stock futures were little changed.

You can point to several reasons why bond yields have been rising — everything from technical reasons related to a greater supply from recent auctions to shifts in the Fed's outlook. Disparate worries about government budget deficits, faster economic growth, and the impact of President-elect Donald Trump's plans to jack up tariffs on imports are also playing a role.

The bottom line is that traders expect inflation in the longer term to be faster than they used to. And that means higher interest rates than previously thought. Fed minutes from December published yesterday revealed officials' concern that it may take longer to get the inflation rate back to the 2% target.

What's more, the bond selloff — yields move inversely to prices — is a global phenomenon. Yields have also been rising in Germany, Japan, and the U.K. The benchmark U.K. 10-year government bond hit its highest yield since 2008, in the midst of the global financial crisis. The 30-year yield is the highest since 1998.

The concern is that British authorities will have to raise taxes or sell more bonds to cover funding shortfalls that are being exacerbated by weak economic growth. That's led to the unusual occurrence of the pound falling even though interest rates are higher — something that was last seen in Prime Minister Liz Truss's short tenure as prime minister in 2022.

It's raising eyebrows in London, but it's not a crisis, at least not yet. And the conundrum of rising yields while the currency drops isn't an issue for the U.S., either. The dollar is still broadly rising — the dollar index, a measure against a basket of currencies, is up 6% over the past three months.

Write to Brian Swint at brian.swint@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Croatia's trade gap up 7.7% y/y in Jan-Nov
data.description:
ZAGREB (Croatia), January 9 (SeeNews) - Croatia's trade deficit expanded to 16.7 billion euro ($17.2 billion) in the first 11 months of last year, compared with a gap of 15.5 billion euro in the prior-year period, the country's statistical office said on Thursday.

Exports rose 3.3% on the year to 21.8 billion euro, while imports increased 5.1% to 38.5 billion euro, the Croatian Bureau of Statistics said in a statement, quoting preliminary data.

Croatia's exports to the European Union (EU) dipped 0.2% year-on-year to 14.3 billion euro, whereas the country's imports from the EU rose by 7.4% to 30.2 billion euro.

($ = 0.970 euro)

data.title: Sweden's CombiGene Cuts All Jobs in Major Restructuring Move
data.description:
CombiGene CCOMBI terminated the employment of its entire workforce as part of a strategic review initiated by its board in December 2024.

The Swedish gene therapy developer said the move enables it to adapt to the current market conditions and reduce fixed costs but does not reflect the employees' performance or competence, according to a Thursday release.

CombiGene plans to prioritize its existing research projects and actively seek partnerships to advance their development. It will also explore new business opportunities, particularly investments in companies with neutral or positive cash flows.

data.title: Mauritius Inflation Rate Slows to 4-Month Low
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in Mauritius eased to a four-month low of 2.9% in December 2024, from 3.4% in each of the previous two months.

Prices slowed down mostly for recreation & culture (2.2% vs 4.1% in November); food & non-alcoholic beverages (7.3% vs 8.1%); miscellaneous goods & services (5.6% vs 6.1%) and furnishings & household equipment (5.3% vs 5.6%).

On a monthly basis, consumer prices fell by 0.5% in December, after a 0.3% increase in the month before.

data.title: National Bank Upgrades i-80 Gold to Outperform From Sector Perform, Adjusts Price Target to $1.22 From $1.63
European Midday Briefing: Surge in Gilt Yields Continues, Raising Concern, as Sterling Falls
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Stocks:

European indices were mixed, with the FTSE 100 posting solid gains after sterling hit a one-year low against the dollar following a sharp selloff in U.K. government bonds.

Elsewhere European shipping companies were under pressure after a strike was averted at major U.S. ports, with Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd both down more than 5%.

Jefferies said freight rates have been boosted recently as retailers increase their stockpile of inventory amid uncertainty surrounding tariffs and the potential port strikes.

"The supply/demand balance remains steady, but a soft patch could come later this year."

Stocks to Watch

The impact of lower interest rates sees a continuation of the challenging operating environment for Nordic banks, Jefferies said separately, as it lowered its EPS estimates by 1%-3% on average across the sector.

"On the macro front, economic sentiment remains weak, and we await an economic recovery in Sweden."

Nordea remains its only buy-rated stock among the Nordic banks, as it continues to see better value elsewhere in the European banking context.

U.S. Markets:

The U.S. stock market will be closed and the bond market will shut early, but Thursday will bring a plethora of timely commentary from Federal Reserve officials as the central bank faces an uncertain inflation outlook.

The Fed's Collins and Harker will both speak around 14:00 GMT, with Barkin, Bowman and Schmid delivering remarks at events later in the day.

The next big impact on rate expectations will be Friday's nonfarm payrolls data.

Forex:

The dollar is likely to remain firm after the Fed minutes confirmed its more cautious stance about cutting interest rates, ING said, adding that the currency is likely to stay strong into Trump's inauguration on January 20.

Sterling could extend its losses--falling below $1.2--on growing investor unease over the ongoing selloff in gilts, MUFG said.

The rise in gilt yields appears broadly in line with similar moves in other bond markets, but rising borrowing costs could put pressure on the government to tighten fiscal policy.

Higher yields due to the Bank of England's caution over cutting interest rates boosted sterling last year.

"Recent price action highlights though, that higher yields are not always positive for a currency if they are driven by unease over the public finances and inflation in the U.K."

Pepperstone said a falling currency and that rising yields were a classic sign of fiscal de-anchoring and of participants losing confidence in the government's ability to exert control over the fiscal backdrop.

It added that it wasn't the same situation as when the 2022 tax-slashing budget under former Prime Minister Liz Truss sparked market turmoil but "things are clearly on very shaky ground" and selling sterling remains its preference.

UOB said the pound is likely to continue its downtrend against the dollar, in coming weeks according to the weekly chart .

Bonds:

Issuance in the eurozone so far has been strongly received, but bond yields keep rising.

"For most of December it was U.S. Treasuries that led the way, since the holidays it has been eurozone government bonds and since yesterday it is U.K. gilts," Commerzbank said.

The rise in Treasury yields in recent weeks has been driven by rising inflation expectations, the repricing of the Fed's interest rates and higher term premium, Metzler said.

"While a rise in the term premium is likely to continue (political uncertainty is likely to remain high under Trump), the mood regarding the Fed, the economy and inflation could quickly change again."

The first signs of that came Wednesday when the Fed's Waller said he expected milder inflation and more rate cuts.

Gilt yields continued to rise--more than other bond yields--and if the current moves continue, it could potentially be evidence of a buyer's strike or capital flight, according to Fidelity.

Energy:

Oil was lower due to a stronger dollar and rise in U.S. fuel stockpiles.

"From a technical perspective, crude oil [WTI] is now in the medium-term bullish consolidation zone and should remain there above the $72.85pb support," Swissquote Bank said.

Prices had a strong start to 2025, supported by colder temperatures boosting heating fuel consumption and worries over Iranian and Russian supplies.

Still, broader concerns of an oversupplied market this year and uncertainties over Chinese growth limit the upside.

Metals:

Gold edged higher despite a stronger dollar, as investors seek safe-haven assets amid growing market uncertainties.

A weaker-than-expected report on U.S. private employment for December provided some reassurance that the Fed might be less cautious about lowering rates.

But the minutes released on Wednesday suggested officials will hold rates steady for now, revealing they saw risks of higher-than-expected inflation partly due to Trump's potential future policies.

"That should hardly come as a surprise given that the median interest rate projection now shows only 50bp in cuts this year," Capital Economics said.

Global Lithium Outlook

Mergers and acquisitions are likely to remain prevalent in the global lithium industry , as valuations decline on expectations for lower lithium prices in the medium term, Goldman Sachs said.

It said the lithium market should be soft again this year as a number of projects ramp up, and reckon producers will be focused on cost-cutting and production efficiency.

EMEA HEADLINES

German Industrial Production Rises Despite Fears of Layoffs, Tariffs

German industrial production ticked up in November last year, offering a glimmer of hope for the beleaguered sector in the face of sweeping job cuts and the threat of import tariffs from the incoming Trump administration.

Output rose 1.5% on month in November, German statistics agency Destatis said Thursday, better than the 0.5% rise expected by economists in a poll compiled by The Wall Street Journal.

China Blasts EU's Foreign Subsidies Law as Unfair Trade Barrier

Beijing has called out the European Union for imposing what it says are unfair trade barriers on Chinese companies, ending a monthslong investigation into the bloc's efforts to protect its businesses from foreign subsidies.

China's commerce ministry released the results of its probe into the EU's Foreign Subsidies Regulation on Thursday, saying that European authorities have implemented the rule selectively in a way that infringes on Chinese companies' interests.

Tesco Sticks to Profit Target After Posting Christmas Sales Growth

Tesco reiterated its profit target for its fiscal year after reporting sales growth during the Christmas period.

The U.K. supermarket chain on Thursday said it continues to expect retail adjusted operating profit-its preferred metric, which strips out exceptional and other one-off items-in the fiscal year ending in February of around 2.9 billion pounds ($3.59 billion).

Marks & Spencer Focused on Growth Plan After Christmas Sales Boost Results

Marks & Spencer said it is confident in making progress in the remainder of the year after it recorded a positive performance during the key Christmas season and as it continues with its growth plan.

The U.K. retailer said Thursday that for the 13 weeks to Dec. 28 it made sales of 4.06 billion pounds ($5.02 billion), up 5.6% compared with the year-earlier period. The result came ahead of analysts' projections of 3.96 billion pounds, according to a Visible Alpha poll of estimates.

GLOBAL NEWS

Trump's Day 1: Incoming president looks set to act on tariffs, crypto, energy and immigration

On his first day back in office, President-elect Donald Trump is planning to go big on executive orders and other actions, including ones that aim to roll back some of his predecessor's policies. It'll be similar to how President Joe Biden rolled out a raft of orders on the day of his inauguration four years ago and targeted a number of Trump 1.0 initiatives.

So which upcoming Trump executive actions on Jan. 20 could have an effect on your investing portfolio or your wallet? Here's a brief guide.

Musk Says DOGE's Goal to Cut $2 Trillion in Spending Is 'Best-Case Outcome'

Elon Musk reiterated that his Department of Government Efficiency will try to cut $2 trillion from federal spending, but he said they may not reach that goal.

"I think if we try for $2 trillion, we've got a good shot at getting one, " Musk said Wednesday in a conversation with Mark Penn, chairman and chief executive of ad company Stagwell, that was livestreamed on Musk's social-media platform X.

Trump's NATO Vision Spells Trouble for the Alliance

President-elect Donald Trump's latest demands of America's NATO partners-that they cede territory to the U.S. and spend more on defense than Washington itself does-risk undermining allies' confidence and potentially emboldening adversaries.

In a press conference Tuesday, Trump raised the prospect of forcibly taking over Canada and Greenland, which is part of Denmark. Canada and Denmark are founding members of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, and the U.S. is treaty-bound to protect them.

Israel's Defense Industry Booms on Foreign Demand and War at Home

TEL AVIV-After a year marked by war and calls for isolating Israel on the world stage, its defense industry is on track to sell more weapons than ever in its history, spurred by foreign demand for its aerial-defense systems and significant government spending aimed at funding Israel's ongoing military campaigns.

The surge in weapons sales for Israel's top three defense companies-among the world's top 10 defense exporters-comes amid widespread arms embargoes on Israel, from even some of its allies. Other countries have suspended their acquisition of Israeli arms over the war in Gaza.

Los Angeles Wildfires Leave at Least Five Dead as Uncontained Blazes Intensify LOS ANGELES-Portions of the heavily populated Hollywood neighborhood were ordered to evacuate Wednesday evening as a new fire broke out in the hills above the center of this city battling numerous deadly blazes.

At least five people were dead and tens of thousands were forced to evacuate as six wildfires simultaneously hit the most populated county in America. More than 2,000 structures were damaged or destroyed, 123,000 people lost power, and the region's firefighting resources were stretched to their limits.

Write to clare.kinloch@wsj.com

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: QST Technologies - New distributor in Singapore and Hong Kong
data.description:
VibroSense

VibroSense Dynamics AB has signed a distributor agreement with QST Technologies in Singapore and Hong Kong. The agreement allows QST Technologies to market and sell the VibroSense Meter ®II in Singapore and Hong Kong within the product's application areas.

QST Technologies has also received a first order for delivery to the Duchess of Kent Children's Hospital at Sandy Bay which is a new customer in Hong Kong. The agreement with QST is a non-exclusive distributor agreement that applies to Singapore and Hong Kong but not to China “Mainland” where UMCARE has an exclusive distributor rights which is valid for three years from the date of receipt of regulatory approval from the Chinese authority NMPA.

“It is gratifying to expand our presence in Asia and now we have the opportunity to make VibroSense Meter® II available to clinics in Singapore and Hong Kong. We have already received our first order from a hospital in Hong Kong via QST Technologies and we look forward to a good collaboration,” says Tarik Cengiz, CEO of VibroSense Dynamics AB.

About Technologies

QST was established in 2005 to provide products and solutions to the healthcare industry. Its customers include medical, dental and veterinary practices. Their expertise lies in providing innovative solutions for the complex and demanding healthcare industry, offering specialized and integrated solutions based on new technological advances to meet the ever-increasing healthcare needs.

With a highly competent and dedicated team of professionals, QST is committed to helping their customers and partners achieve beyond their goals. The business growth has been built upon trust and reliability, with offices situated in Singapore, Malaysia and Hong Kong and a growing sales network spanning Asia.

Contact

Tarik Cengiz , CEO VibroSense Dynamics AB

Tel: +46 40 88 026

Email: info@vibrosense.com

www.vibrosense.se

"The new Gold Standard for reliabledetection of nerve damage"

About VibroSense Dynamics AB ( publ )

VibroSense Dynamics AB (publ) develops and sells medical technology products and services for diagnostic support for nerve damage in the hands and feet. The method involves measuring and quantifying the ability to feel vibrations at several frequencies. The company's customers are diabetes clinics, occupational health care, hospitals, health centers and researchers.

Our vision is for the company's products to be a standard instrument in all neurological examinations to detect early signs of changes in sensation so that patients and their caregivers can implement preventive measures that prevent, reduce or delay the onset of nerve damage in the hands and feet.

https://news.cision.com/vibrosense/r/qst-technologies---new-distributor-in-singapore-and-hong-kong%2Cc4089449

QST Technologies_New_distributor_eng_A

data.title: Brits are getting healthier but not happier, top retailers say
data.description:
By Steve Goldstein

Tesco, Greggs and M&S point to restrained U.K. consumer

Brits are getting healthier but not happier, according to updates from three of the company's biggest food outlets.

Tesco tracks purchases made using its loyalty card, and for the last two years its most-popular lunchtime meal deal combo was a combination of a sausage, bacon and egg sandwich, grilled steak flavor potato chips, and a can of Coke. But now the chicken club is the top sandwich, and the egg protein pot is the top snack - Coke remains the drink choice with that package.

That news comes as Tesco reported a 3.8% rise in same-store sales over the Christmas period covering the six weeks ending Jan. 4. That's an acceleration of the 2.8% same-store sales growth in the fiscal third quarter ending Nov. 23.

Tesco shares (UK:TSCO) slipped 2% on the session, even as the sales came in ahead of estimates. Tesco reiterated its guidance on operating profit and cash flow from its retail operations.

"Tesco is well set to face into the ongoing U.K. consumer economy, where sentiment is rather weak and cost pressures evident. We like the ongoing investment thesis of a cash compounder albeit sentiment and momentum are a little sideways today, but the stock could be a notable relative winner as matters unfold," said Clive Black, an analyst at U.K. broker Clive Black.

That strained sentiment was seen at Greggs, a U.K. bakery chain, where its comparable-store sales in the fourth quarter rose 2.5%, slower than the 5% growth in the third quarter and the 7.4% growth in the first half.

Greggs, which generated fanfare in the U.K. when it added vegan sausage rolls to its menu, said lower consumer confidence impacted customer traffic but said it retained customer market share, including being the number-one destination for breakfast.

Greggs (UK:GRG) also reiterated its outlook, as the stock tumbled 11% to a new 52-week low.

Greggs said "employment costs will result in further overall cost inflation, although wage increases should provide support to consumers." That's a reference not just to rising wages in the country but also the planned increase of employer-side national-insurance taxes.

Marks & Spencer shares (UK:MKS) fell 5% as the U.K. retailer said its food comparable-store sales jumped 8.9% while clothing sales rose 1.9%.

M&S shares still are up 50% from their lows of March. "The external environment remains challenging, with cost and economic headwinds to navigate, but there is much within our control," CEO Stuart Machin said in a statement.

-Steve Goldstein

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: AUDUSD Technical Analysis – The pair bounces around the 2022 lows
data.description:
Fundamental Overview

The USD got a boost recently from another set of hot data as the US Job Openings surprised to the upside and the prices paid index in the ISM Services PMI jumped to the highest level since 2023.

The market’s pricing didn’t change much though and Fed’s Waller yesterday kept the rate cuts hopes alive saying that the pace will be driven by inflation progress. Since the central bank has switched its focus back to inflation, the US CPI report next week should have a bigger influence on interest rates expectations than the US NFP tomorrow (barring big deviations).

On the AUD side, the RBA softened further its stance at the last policy decision as it nears the first rate cut. The market is seeing a 57% chance of a 25 bps cut in February although the first fully priced in cut is seen in April.

The Australian Monthly CPI yesterday showed core inflation easing with the Trimmed Mean CPI Y/Y coming in at 3.2%. The RBA’s inflation target band is 2-3%.

AUDUSD Technical Analysis – Daily Timeframe

AUDUSD Daily

On the daily chart, we can see that AUDUSD is bouncing from the 2022 lows. This is where the buyers are stepping in with a defined risk below the lows to position for a rally back into the 0.69 handle. The sellers, on the other hand, will want to see the price breaking lower to increase the bearish bets into new lows.

AUDUSD Technical Analysis – 4 hour Timeframe

AUDUSD 4 hour

On the 4 hour chart, we can see that from a risk management perspective, the sellers will have a better risk to reward setup around the major trendline and the 0.6270 resistance. The buyers, on the other hand, will want to see the price breaking above that zone to gain even more conviction and increase the bullish bets into new highs.

AUDUSD Technical Analysis – 1 hour Timeframe

AUDUSD 1 hour

On the 1 hour chart, we can see that we have a minor downward trendline defining the current bearish momentum on this timeframe. The sellers will likely keep on leaning on it to position for the break below the lows, while the buyers will look for a break higher to increase the bullish bets into the 0.6270 resistance. The red lines define the average daily range for today.

Upcoming Catalysts

Tomorrow, we conclude the week with the US NFP report.

data.title: Storytel, Vodafone Turkey Team Up for Audio Stories
data.description:
Storytel (STORY-B.ST) will collaborate with Vodafone's VOD Turkish arm to offer its audiobooks and e-books to the latter's subscribers.

The deal, which aims to boost the Swedish audiobook and e-book company's footprint in Turkey, can be accessed through mobile payments, according to a Thursday release.

data.title: National Bank Financial Adjusts Price Target on Newmont to CA$69 From CA$72, Keeps Sector Perform Rating
Shein aims for London IPO by mid-year, sources say
data.description:
Key points:
  • UK officials' visit to China could progress approvals -source
  • London IPO could come as early as Easter -source
  • Definitive timeline still in flux -source

Online fast-fashion retailer Shein is aiming to list in London in the first half of the year, according to two sources with direct knowledge of the matter, assuming it gains regulatory approvals for the initial public offering.

The IPO could be completed as early as Easter, which is April 20, one of the people said.

A visit to China by Britain's finance minister Rachel Reeves starting on Saturday, during which she will meet with Premier He Lifeng to discuss economic and financial cooperation, could help progress the regulatory approvals Shein needs, the source added.

A second person with knowledge of the matter said Shein, founded in China in 2012, is working towards listing in the first half of this year, but the definitive timeline is still in flux.

The London listing push comes after the company ended its attempt at a U.S. IPO after pushback from lawmakers concerned about risks connected to China and alleged labour malpractices.

The head of Britain's Financial Conduct Authority, which is in charge of assessing and approving flotations like Shein's IPO, is accompanying Reeves on the trip to Beijing and Shanghai and will meet with regulatory partners there.

Shein declined to comment, the FCA said it does not comment on potential listing applications, and Britain's finance ministry did not reply to Reuters' questions.

Even though it moved its headquarters from Nanjing to Singapore in 2022, Shein also requires permission from the China Securities Regulatory Commission, making it subject to offshore listing rules, as most of its 5,800 contract manufacturers are in China.

New rules passed by the CSRC in 2023 allow it to vet and potentially block offshore listings.

The CSRC did not immediately reply to questions about Britain's visit and Shein's IPO.

Shein is walking a political tightrope as it tries to show it has measures in place to limit the risk of human rights violations in its supply chain while avoiding any direct claims about China's Xinjiang province - a top cotton-producing region where the United States and NGOs have accused the government of forced labour and other abuses against Uyghur people.

Beijing denies any abuses, and Chinese authorities have hit back at clothing brands that say they don't use Xinjiang cotton.

Shein's general counsel for Europe, the Middle East and Africa, Yinan Zhu, on Tuesday declined to directly answer when asked by a British parliamentary committee whether the retailer's clothes contain cotton from China or Xinjiang, or whether it tells suppliers not to source from the province.

Zhu asked instead to provide the committee with written answers, and said Shein complies with relevant laws in all jurisdictions.

data.title: COMMENT-Labour isn't working for pound
data.description:
Jan 9 (Reuters) - A crisis of confidence in Britain's fiscal outlook six months after Labour's landslide UK election victory has left sterling bulls discontent with the winter performance of the pound.

Rachel Reeves is facing her first major test as Britain's finance minister after the UK government's borrowing costs jumped sharply this week, potentially forcing her to cut future spending.

This week's rise in gilt yields has negatively impacted the pound - with GBP/USD sinking to a 14-month low of 1.2239 and GBP/EUR sliding to a two-month low a fraction shy of 1.1900 on Thursday.

Higher gilt yields also hurt GBP following the tax-and-spend budget unveiled by Reeves in her maiden budget on Oct. 30.

A UK Treasury minister has been called to parliament on Thursday to answer an urgent question about "the growing pressure of borrowing costs on the public finances". On Wednesday, the Treasury said its commitment to the UK government's fiscal rules was "non-negotiable".

data.title: REC to issue 10-year bonds, bankers say
data.description:
India's REC RECLTD plans to raise 40 billion rupees ($465.92 million), including a greenshoe option of 33 billion rupees, through the sale of bonds maturing in 10 years, three merchant bankers said on Thursday.

REC has invited coupon and commitment bids from bankers and investors on Monday, they said.

The company did not immediately reply to a Reuters email seeking comment.

data.title: U.S. Adds ByteDance Unit, Drops Tencent's WeChat From 'Notorious Markets' List
data.description:
By Sherry Qin

The U.S. Trade Representative has added an e-commerce platform owned by TikTok's parent to its "notorious markets" list, naming the entity as one of more than 70 online and physical markets potentially engaging in or facilitating counterfeiting and piracy.

The office of the USTR said in a statement dated Wednesday that the updated list reflected "the evolving nature of counterfeit sales in China" by including ByteDance's Douyin Mall for the first time along with several Chinese entities previously on the list, including Alibaba's Taobao, fast-growing online retailer Pinduoduo and the cloud-storage service of tech company Baidu, among others.

Douyin Mall, the e-commerce platform of Douyin--the Chinese version of ByteDance's TikTok platform--made the list for the first time due to a "'rocketing' increase in the amount of counterfeit goods on the platform," the USTR said.

WeChat, the popular messaging app of Chinese tech giant Tencent that had been on the list since 2021, didn't appear this year, suggesting its removal.

The list, published annually since 2011, identifies online and physical markets that allegedly engage in "substantial trademark counterfeiting or copyright piracy," with an aim of raising public awareness and helping market operators and governments "prioritize intellectual property enforcement efforts." The list doesn't reflect findings of legal violations, the USTR said.

ByteDance didn't immediately respond to a request for comment. Danny Marti, head of public affairs and global policy at Tencent, said he was "pleased to see recognition of Tencent's commitment to intellectual property protection."

Citi analyst Alicia Yap viewed Tencent's removal as "a positive read-through on Tencent's product quality." More importantly, the move suggests that U.S. regulators aren't systematically targeting specific companies like Tencent, she added in a research note. That means the tech giant could eventually succeed in its current effort to obtain removal from a separate Pentagon list of Chinese companies with alleged military ties released earlier this week.

Write to Sherry Qin at sherry.qin@wsj.com
